In your set up here though there are two process at work1) Coherer effect 2) Preferential discharge between two different capacitances, watch carefully the explosion gets louder progressively.

author: @littleshopofelectrons4014
text: Its pretty simple. The water has a much lower resistance than your spark gap. The current will take the path of least resistance. Once plasma forms in the water, this provides multiple conductive paths for the current.

author: @navneetpandit7299
text: Nothing wierd here, this is called as the coherer effect, the hylem board with the water on it gets conducting at High-Voltage, the same effect they use in producing electric 'treeing' on wodden plank ect. to produce artistic creation. The Insulation wears out and turns the low-capaciance polarization interface between the plates (holding the spark -gap in your set up.) into higher capciance usually carbonizing and de-tunning the High-Voltage source. And yes those capacitors there enhance the action of polarization i just described above.

author: @___Chris___
text:
The relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of water is about 80times higher than for air, which is why I would assume that seeing the water exploding at the bottom is just ionization (followed by current flow) following the path of least resistance.
Btw: do you know if this "water explosion" works just the same way in dielectric barrier discharge, with one exposed and one covered electrode or do for some reason both electrodes have to be exposed? It should in theory, but maybe you've tried it(?).
Sorry, I know this video is incredibly old - I'm just trying to follow up with what you've done in the past, because your channel can't be overestimated as a source of inspiration. I greatly appreciate how much work you put into it over all these years, with hundreds of videos. You deserve millions of subscribers.

author: @RadioTrefoil
text:
When capacitors are in parallel their capacitance increases and voltage stays the same. You have 2000 volts at 5uF, not 10,000 volts. You are getting confused with series capacitors. However, even if they were in series, you would still only getting 2000 volts because that's the maximum voltage achievable from a MOT (unless you charged them in parallel and discharged them in series, which would require a rather complex mechanism).

As for the exploding water phenomenon, I guess the same effect would occur if you didn't have a spark gap at all (just discharging the capacitor directly into water). A high power arc, such as a capacitor discharge, probably just heats the water to a high temperature very quickly, causing a steam explosion (too little total electrical energy to produce hydrogen and oxygen; the capacitors are only discharging at 5 joules). It doesn't look as if the spark gap you have here is actually doing anything. Of course, 2000 volts isn't a high enough for the spark gap to fire anyway, although I can see why you thought that it was firing if you thought it was 10,000 volts.

author: @ravemaster2000
text: try it on plastic Robert it could be a discovery, conductive plastic. I remember I got my stun gun wet one time, Enough to make the electricity travel across the wet part and not the spark gap. and I shocked the water until evaporated and electricity was still traveling not across the spark gap, but across the ionized path that the water created. off topic But I thought that that was interesting that if i put water on something that is not conductive and you zap it with high-voltage it may make a conductive path. The way I understand electricity is that it needs to build up Just enough pressure to jump the easiest pass to the other side And that would be the water in this case because it would require less pressure in order to travel across the water instead of the spark gap

author: @m3sca1
text:
i have done similar exploding water experiments-there is a lot more energy when water is present than when it is not.
it ticks all the boxes for me in regards to fun experimentation.
bright lights
loud bangs
high voltage
and mysterious results
i dont think path of least resistance is a good enough answer for this one.
the gap is much closer than the distance on the wet board,i think the water polarises and becomes conductive as the hydrogen and oxygen are ripped apart.
then it all burns and goes back together and becomes water again

author: @IlluminatedWhiteGuy
text: The water has less resistance then the air between the spark gap so the energy takes the path of least resistance.  The water is not burning, it is completing the circuit and creating plasma from the discharge. I'm quite sure if you were careful and didn't spray the entire device down with water and only applied it on the board beneath it would spark at the gap. As the electricity has a direct path when you soak the whole device one would normally expect just what is happening.
